claims.evidence_text: A harvest objective with current varieties grown in Virginia should be 60-70 heads/sq. ft. with at least 30 kernels/head.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat harvest objective in Virginia is 60-70 heads per square foot (unit as stated: heads/sq ft).

claims.evidence_text: A harvest objective with current varieties grown in Virginia should be 60-70 heads/sq. ft. with at least 30 kernels/head.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat harvest objective is at least 30 kernels per head.

claims.evidence_text: a moderate amount (15-30 lbs. of N/acre) is all that is needed for establishing a timely planted winter wheat crop.
claims.claim_summary: Autumn N for timely-planted winter wheat: a moderate 15-30 lb N/acre to establish the crop and promote fall tillers.

claims.evidence_text: Apply 15 to 30 lbs. N/acre just before planting to stimulate tillering and root development in those situations where residual N availability is less than desired. The N fertilizer should be incorporated in the surface 2 to 4 inches for conventional tillage systems.
claims.claim_summary: Apply 15-30 lb N/acre just before planting where residual N is low; incorporate into surface 2-4 inches for conventional tillage, surface application adequate for no-till.

claims.evidence_text: Nitrogen applications during this period should not exceed 60 lbs. N/acre if split-spring N applications are planned. Applications greater than 60 lbs. N/acre during late winter have not increased yields
claims.claim_summary: Late-winter/early-spring N should not exceed 60 lb N/acre when split-spring applications are planned; higher rates did not increase yield.

claims.evidence_text: Total spring N applications (growth stage 25 plus growth stage 30) should not exceed a total of 120 lbs. N/acre in order to avoid problems with lodging and yield loss.
claims.claim_summary: Total spring N (GS25 + GS30) should not exceed 120 lb N/acre to avoid lodging and yield loss; up to 120 lb/acre at GS30 if none applied at GS25.

claims.evidence_text: If tiller numbers are low, 50/sq. ft. or less, N fertilization at this time is critical... If tiller numbers are high, 100/sq. ft. or more, no N application is needed at this time.
claims.claim_summary: Tiller density thresholds at spring greenup (GS25): <=50 tillers/sq ft is low (N critical), >=100 tillers/sq ft is high (no N needed); 90/sq ft delays single application to GS30.

claims.evidence_text: a single early application will result in too many tillers, leading to spindly shoots, too many leaves, and increased probability of disease and lodging.
claims.claim_summary: Excessive/early spring N produces too many tillers and spindly shoots, increasing disease and lodging probability in wheat.

claims.evidence_text: Foliar applications (10 to 20 lbs. N/acre) of urea at this growth stage have been shown to increase grain protein but not yield.
claims.claim_summary: Foliar urea (10-20 lb N/acre) at/after flowering increases wheat grain protein but not yield.

claims.evidence_text: if soil nitrate levels are less than 10 ppm NO3--N in the top 6 inches of soil, the emerging seedlings are likely to exhibit N deficiency symptoms. Extractable nitrate levels of 30 ppm NO3--N in the surface 6 inches... indicate a high level of N availability and no fertilizer N application is required.
claims.claim_summary: Soil nitrate <10 ppm NO3-N in top 6 in causes wheat seedling N deficiency; 30 ppm indicates high availability and no fall N needed.

claims.evidence_text: an expectation for the specific site that several growing days (temperatures of 50 degrees F or greater) will occur in January and early February.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat growing days require temperatures of 50 F or greater; used to judge whether January N application will be utilized.
